title: Laying glue-down vinyl plank flooring
subtitle: Adhere LVT planks directly to a clean, level subfloor
excerpt: How to lay luxury vinyl tile (LVT) planks using full-spread adhesive on a prepared subfloor. Covers surface preparation, adhesive open time, plank direction and layout, and the rolling pass that beds the planks fully into the adhesive.
sourceNotes: BS 8203: Code of practice for installation of resilient floor coverings. Contract Flooring Association (CFA) technical guidance on LVT installation. Amtico and Karndean installation notes (referenced, not reproduced).

glossaryTerms.term: LVT (luxury vinyl tile or plank), glue-down
glossaryTerms.definition: Luxury vinyl tile or plank flooring fixed directly to the subfloor with a pressure-sensitive or full-bond adhesive. Unlike click-lock LVT (floating), glue-down LVT has no locking mechanism — each plank is held entirely by the adhesive. More stable underfoot and better suited to commercial and high-traffic domestic use. Cannot be floated over an underfloor-heating screed without the manufacturer's approval.

The period after spreading a floor adhesive during which it remains workable and tiles or planks can be pressed into it and repositioned. Pressure-sensitive adhesive develops a skin as the solvent flashes off: once the surface turns from shiny to tacky and matte, the plank must be pressed in immediately. Laying outside the open time causes poor adhesion and early failure.

content.text: is adhered directly to the subfloor with a contact or pressure-sensitive adhesive rather than relying on a click-lock edge joint. The result is more stable underfoot, with no springiness or hollow sound when walked on. The entire job depends on the subfloor being flat to within 3 mm over a 1.8 m straight-edge: any high spots cause the plank to rock and eventually crack the bond; any dips cause hollow-sounding sections.

LVT planks (measure the room and add 10% for cuts and waste). Full-spread floor adhesive specified by the LVT manufacturer; do not substitute. A notched trowel (notch size specified on the adhesive tin, typically 1 to 2 mm V-notch for thin-bed LVT). A 35 to 45 kg roller for bedding. A utility knife with a fresh hook blade or a guillotine trimmer for cutting planks. A 1.8 m straight-edge. Chalk line for layout. A tape measure.
Step 1, prepare and check the subfloor

The subfloor must be clean, dry, smooth, and free from movement. Check for high spots with the straight-edge and sand them down; check for dips and fill them with a floor-levelling compound (allow to cure fully). The subfloor moisture content must be below 75% relative humidity (use a hygrometer on concrete; use a meter probe on timber). New concrete screed must be allowed a minimum of one month of drying time per 25 mm of depth. Remove all old adhesive residue and surface contaminants.
Step 2, acclimatise the planks and set out

Leave the unopened boxes of LVT in the room for at least 24 hours (48 hours in winter) before laying, to allow the planks to reach room temperature and stabilise dimensionally. Set out the layout using chalk lines: find the centre of the room and work from the centre outward, adjusting until any cut planks at opposite walls are of equal width. Run planks parallel to the longest wall or the direction of incoming light unless the manufacturer specifies otherwise.

Spread the adhesive over the subfloor using the notched trowel at the coverage rate stated on the tin; typically 4 to 6 square metres at a time. Spread in one direction only so the adhesive ribs are parallel. Allow the adhesive to develop the correct

; this varies by product and room temperature but is typically 10 to 30 minutes. The surface should look matte and feel tacky rather than wet and shiny when it is ready. Work in sections that you can cover within the open time.
Step 4, lay and roll the planks

Press each plank firmly into the adhesive, starting from the centre line and working outward. Butt planks end-to-end with joints offset by at least 150 mm (one-third of the plank length) between rows, as in a brick-bond pattern. Once a section is laid, roll the entire area with the 35 to 45 kg roller; first in the plank direction, then across it. Rolling eliminates air pockets and ensures full adhesive contact across each plank. Any plank not rolled within 15 minutes of laying may not be fully bonded.

attrs.symptom: Planks lifting at the edges within days
attrs.cause: Adhesive was applied outside the open time window (too wet or too dry), or planks were not rolled.
attrs.fix: Lift the affected planks, clean the adhesive from both surfaces, re-apply fresh adhesive, and relay. Roll immediately after laying.

attrs.symptom: Hollow sound when walking across the laid floor
attrs.cause: Adhesive did not achieve full coverage; subfloor dips not filled before laying.
attrs.fix: Apply firm pressure to the hollow plank — if it bonds with pressure, re-roll with additional weight. If the hollow persists, the plank may need to be lifted, the subfloor high spot or dip addressed, and the plank re-laid.
